 succeeded in 1129ms:
1:"""M-D8 phase 1 v1 (Phase D): Parallel-fetch substrate.
2:
3:The existing live retrieval pipeline (`src/polaris_graph/
4:retrieval/live_retriever.py`) issues one fetch at a time
5:across heterogeneous backends (Serper, Semantic Scholar,
6:DuckDuckGo, etc.). M-D8 phase 1 ships the **parallel-fetch
7:substrate** that callers can wire up to those backends to
8:fan out N fetches concurrently, with per-backend
9:concurrency limits.
10:
11:## Why this milestone matters
12:
13:For predictable workloads where the URL list is known
14:ahead of time (cache warming via M-D7 phase 2, batch
15:audit re-runs, scheduled freshness checks), serial fetching
16:pays first-byte latency on every URL. Phase 1 ships the
17:substrate primitive that turns that serial loop into a
18:concurrent fan-out with operator-controllable concurrency.
19:
20:Phase 1 does NOT integrate with `live_retriever.py` itself
21:— that's phase 2 territory and requires touching production
22:retrieval flow. v1 here is the deterministic substrate the
23:integration will sit on top of.
24:
25:## What v1 ships
26:
27:  - `ParallelFetcher` Protocol — pluggable per-task fetcher
28:  - `FetchTask` dataclass — source_url + backend_id
29:  - `FetchOutcome` enum: SUCCESS | ERRORED | TIMEOUT
30:  - `FetchResultRecord` per-task outcome (renamed from
31:    FetchResult to avoid collision with M-D7 phase 2's
32:    `cache_warming.FetchResult`)
33:  - `ParallelFetchReport` aggregate
34:  - `parallel_fetch(tasks, fetcher, *, max_workers,
35:    per_backend_max_concurrent, per_task_timeout)` —
36:    pure substrate
37:
38:## Substrate boundary
39:
40:Imports stdlib only (`concurrent.futures`, `threading`,
41:`dataclasses`, `enum`). No HTTP client. No DB. No LLM. The
42:Fetcher Protocol is the seam: caller code wires up actual
43:HTTP / Crossref / Semantic Scholar clients.
44:
45:The substrate uses `ThreadPoolExecutor` for parallelism
46:(stdlib, no async/await refactor on the caller side). Per-
47:backend concurrency limits are enforced via
48:`threading.Semaphore` instances keyed by `backend_id`.
49:
50:See `docs/md8_phase1_threat_model.md` for boundaries.
51:"""
52:
53:from __future__ import annotations
54:
55:import concurrent.futures
56:import math
57:import os
58:import threading
59:import time
60:from dataclasses import dataclass, field
61:from enum import Enum
62:from typing import Mapping, Protocol, Sequence
63:
64:
65:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66:# Errors
67:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
68:
69:
70:class ParallelFetchError(ValueError):
71:    """Raised on contract violations — empty tasks not actually
72:    empty, bad fetcher Protocol, invalid concurrency limits."""
73:
74:
75:class FetcherProtocolError(ParallelFetchError):
76:    """Fetcher returned something other than a FetchResultRecord
77:    payload tuple. Caller-side bug — surface loudly per LAW II."""
78:
79:
80:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
81:# Task + outcome types
82:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
83:
84:
85:@dataclass(frozen=True)
86:class FetchTask:
87:    """One unit of work for the parallel fetcher.
88:
89:    `source_url` is the URL to fetch (caller's responsibility
90:    to canonicalize if needed — substrate doesn't normalize).
91:
92:    `backend_id` is the rate-limit class — Serper, Semantic
93:    Scholar, DuckDuckGo, etc. Per-backend concurrency limits
94:    use this as the semaphore key. Tasks with no specific
95:    backend should use a sentinel like `"default"`.
96:
97:    `task_metadata` is opaque caller-supplied metadata
98:    surfaced back in `FetchResultRecord.task_metadata` for
99:    correlation. Common usage: tier classification, domain
100:    tag, request-id-for-tracing. The substrate doesn't read
101:    or interpret this dict.
102:    """
103:
104:    source_url: str
105:    backend_id: str
106:    task_metadata: Mapping[str, object] = field(default_factory=dict)
107:
108:
109:class FetchOutcome(str, Enum):
110:    """Per-task outcome.
111:
112:    SUCCESS: fetcher returned a payload (regardless of HTTP
113:       status code — caller's Fetcher decides what to raise on).
114:    ERRORED: fetcher raised an exception.
115:    TIMEOUT: per-task timeout fired AFTER the task STARTED (it
116:       reached `fetcher.fetch` and over-ran its own start-anchored
117:       budget — or the global batch budget fired while it was
118:       in-flight past its deadline).
119:    NOT_DISPATCHED: the global batch budget (I-fetch-003) fired
120:       while this task was still QUEUED and had never started — the
121:       worker pool was starved by abandoned in-flight fetches, so
122:       this task never reached `fetcher.fetch`. Distinct from
123:       TIMEOUT (which means the task DID run): NOT_DISPATCHED is a
124:       never-ran non-result. Downstream consumers treat it as a
125:       non-result, the same class as TIMEOUT/ERRORED (no payload).
126:    """
127:
128:    SUCCESS = "success"
129:    ERRORED = "errored"
130:    TIMEOUT = "timeout"
131:    NOT_DISPATCHED = "not_dispatched"
132:
133:
134:@dataclass(frozen=True)
135:class FetchResultRecord:
136:    """One task's result.
137:
138:    `payload` is None unless `outcome == SUCCESS`. Type is
139:    deliberately `bytes | None` — substrate doesn't dictate
140:    whether the caller's Fetcher returns text/JSON/binary;
141:    bytes is the lowest-common-denominator transport.
142:
143:    `content_type` and `fetch_status_code` are caller-supplied
144:    metadata when SUCCESS; None otherwise.
145:
146:    `error` is None unless `outcome == ERRORED` or `TIMEOUT`,
147:    in which case it carries `str(exc)` (ERRORED) or the
148:    string `"per-task timeout exceeded"` (TIMEOUT). Exception
149:    type is NOT preserved — callers wanting structured errors
150:    should make their Fetcher catch + convert.
151:
152:    `started_at` / `finished_at` are UNIX epoch floats. For
153:    SUCCESS / ERRORED they mark when the task began (after
154:    backend semaphore acquired) and ended. For TIMEOUT,
155:    `started_at` is best-effort actual task start if the
156:    worker reached the fetch path; otherwise it falls back to
157:    the submit-time timeout-budget anchor (a task can time out
158:    before entering `fetcher.fetch` under contention).
159:
160:    `task_metadata` echoes the FetchTask's metadata for
161:    correlation.
162:    """
163:
164:    source_url: str
165:    backend_id: str
166:    outcome: FetchOutcome
167:    payload: bytes | None
168:    content_type: str | None
169:    fetch_status_code: int | None
170:    error: str | None
171:    started_at: float
172:    finished_at: float
173:    task_metadata: Mapping[str, object] = field(default_factory=dict)
174:
175:
176:@dataclass(frozen=True)
177:class ParallelFetchReport:
178:    """Aggregate of all tasks in one parallel_fetch call.
179:
180:    `started_at` / `finished_at` are wall-clock bounds of the
181:    full call (NOT sum of per-task times — they're concurrent).
182:
183:    `results` is one entry per UNIQUE (source_url, backend_id)
184:    in the input. Duplicates collapse to first-occurrence
185:    (the substrate doesn't fetch the same URL twice in one
186:    call by design).
187:
188:    `not_dispatched_count` (I-fetch-003) counts tasks the global
189:    batch budget cancelled while still QUEUED (never started) —
190:    see `FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED`. It is its own bucket so the
191:    invariant below stays honest.
192:
193:    Counts sum to `len(results)`:
194:    `success_count + errored_count + timeout_count
195:    + not_dispatched_count == len(results)`.
196:    """
197:
198:    started_at: float
199:    finished_at: float
200:    results: tuple[FetchResultRecord, ...] = field(default_factory=tuple)
201:    success_count: int = 0
202:    errored_count: int = 0
203:    timeout_count: int = 0
204:    not_dispatched_count: int = 0
205:
206:
207:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
208:# Fetcher Protocol
209:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
210:
211:
212:class ParallelFetcher(Protocol):
213:    """Pluggable fetcher contract — single-task, called
214:    concurrently by `parallel_fetch`.
215:
216:    Implementers MUST:
217:      - Be thread-safe (concurrent invocations from worker
218:        threads are the norm)
219:      - Return a `tuple[bytes, str, int]` of (payload,
220:        content_type, fetch_status_code) on success
221:      - Raise an exception on transport / parse failure
222:        (callers should NOT signal failure via a non-2xx
223:        status code in the returned tuple — that IS a
224:        successful fetch of an error response)
225:
226:    Implementers MUST NOT:
227:      - Mutate any cache directly (that's caller orchestration)
228:      - Block the calling thread indefinitely (use the
229:        per-task timeout if your backend doesn't enforce one)
230:    """
231:
232:    def fetch(
233:        self, task: FetchTask
234:    ) -> tuple[bytes, str, int]:
235:        ...
236:
237:
238:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
239:# Default per-backend limit
240:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
241:
242:
243:# Conservative default — any backend not explicitly configured
244:# in `per_backend_max_concurrent` gets this limit. Operators
245:# can tune for known backends (Semantic Scholar = 1 per their
246:# docs; Serper = 10; etc.) by passing a dict.
247:DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT = 4
248:
249:
250:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
251:# Harvest-loop poll interval (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01)
252:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
253:
254:
255:# Upper bound on a single harvest-loop wait. The deadline is anchored at
256:# each task's OBSERVED start (`task_started_monotonic_by_index`), not submit,
257:# so a task that has not started yet can never be marked TIMEOUT. To detect
258:# a task that STARTS after the previous snapshot (or one that hangs) within
259:# a bounded delay of its own start-anchored budget, the harvest loop never
260:# waits unbounded: it re-snapshots start-times + recomputes the expired set

 succeeded in 1148ms:
260:# waits unbounded: it re-snapshots start-times + recomputes the expired set
261:# at least this often. Small enough to be responsive, large enough to avoid
262:# a busy-poll. The actual wait is `min(next_deadline - now, this)`.
263:_HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS = 2.0
264:
265:
266:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
267:# Global batch-budget termination guarantee (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01 P1)
268:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
269:
270:
271:# The per-task START-anchored timeout guarantees a STARTED task terminates,
272:# but a never-started task carries a +inf deadline: if every worker thread is
273:# wedged on an abandoned (timed-out but un-cancellable) in-flight fetch, the
274:# queued siblings keep their +inf deadline and the harvest loop could spin
275:# forever. The GLOBAL batch budget is the backstop that guarantees
276:# `parallel_fetch` ALWAYS returns in bounded time even under all-workers-wedged
277:# + queued-siblings. When the budget fires, every still-remaining future is
278:# recorded — started-but-unfinished as TIMEOUT (it ran, over-ran its budget),
279:# never-started as NOT_DISPATCHED (it never reached the fetcher) — and the
280:# harvest loop breaks.
281:#
282:# Operators override the budget directly with this env var (seconds). When
283:# UNSET, the budget is DERIVED from `per_task_timeout` and the number of
284:# submission waves: `per_task_timeout * (ceil(len(deduped)/max_workers)
285:# + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK)`. `ceil(len/max_workers)` is the number of waves
286:# needed to run every task once if each task takes a full per_task_timeout; the
287:# slack adds head-room so a healthy batch (tasks finishing well inside budget,
288:# semaphore queuing) is never tripped spuriously.
289:PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS_ENV = (
290:    "PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS"
291:)
292:_BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK = 2
293:
294:
295:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
296:# Round-robin submit ordering (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C02)
297:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
298:
299:
300:def _round_robin_indices(
301:    tasks: Sequence[FetchTask],
302:) -> list[int]:
303:    """Return ORIGINAL task indices reordered into round-robin
304:    `backend_id` order.
305:
306:    Group indices by `backend_id` (preserving first-seen group order
307:    and intra-group input order), then emit one index per backend per
308:    round until every group is exhausted. So the first ``len(groups)``
309:    indices span distinct backends/hosts.
310:
311:    The result is a permutation of ``range(len(tasks))`` — every input
312:    index appears exactly once — so callers map results back to the
313:    ORIGINAL index unchanged. Pure / deterministic / allocation-light.
314:    """
315:    groups: dict[str, list[int]] = {}
316:    for idx, task in enumerate(tasks):
317:        groups.setdefault(task.backend_id, []).append(idx)
318:
319:    ordered: list[int] = []
320:    group_queues = list(groups.values())
321:    while group_queues:
322:        next_round: list[list[int]] = []
323:        for queue in group_queues:
324:            ordered.append(queue.pop(0))
325:            if queue:
326:                next_round.append(queue)
327:        group_queues = next_round
328:    return ordered
329:
330:
331:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
332:# Global batch-budget derivation (I-fetch-003 / BB5-C01 P1)
333:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
334:
335:
336:def _derive_batch_budget_seconds(
337:    *,
338:    num_tasks: int,
339:    max_workers: int,
340:    per_task_timeout: float | None,
341:) -> float | None:
342:    """Derive the GLOBAL batch budget (seconds) or None to disable it.
343:
344:    Precedence:
345:      1. Env `PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS` (positive float) wins.
346:      2. Else, if `per_task_timeout` is set: derive from the wave count —
347:         `per_task_timeout * (ceil(num_tasks / max_workers)
348:         + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK)`. The ceil term is how many sequential
349:         waves it takes to run every task once if each consumes a full
350:         per_task_timeout; the slack is head-room so a healthy batch is never
351:         tripped spuriously.
352:      3. Else (no env AND no per-task timeout): None — preserve the existing
353:         "None disables timeout" contract. With no per-task budget there is no
354:         natural time-scale to derive from; the caller opted out of bounding.
355:
356:    An env value that is empty, non-numeric, or <= 0 is ignored (falls through
357:    to the derived/None path) — a malformed knob must never silently disable
358:    the termination guarantee when a per_task_timeout is in force.
359:    """
360:    env_raw = os.environ.get(PG_PARALLEL_FETCH_BATCH_BUDGET_SECONDS_ENV)
361:    if env_raw is not None and env_raw.strip():
362:        try:
363:            env_val = float(env_raw)
364:        except ValueError:
365:            env_val = None
366:        if env_val is not None and env_val > 0:
367:            return env_val
368:
369:    if per_task_timeout is None:
370:        return None
371:
372:    waves = math.ceil(num_tasks / max_workers) + _BATCH_BUDGET_WAVE_SLACK
373:    return per_task_timeout * waves
374:
375:
376:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
377:# Public API
378:# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
379:
380:
381:def parallel_fetch(
382:    tasks: Sequence[FetchTask],
383:    fetcher: ParallelFetcher,
384:    *,
385:    max_workers: int = 8,
386:    per_backend_max_concurrent: Mapping[str, int] | None = None,
387:    per_task_timeout: float | None = None,
388:) -> ParallelFetchReport:
389:    """Fan out `tasks` to a thread pool and collect results.
390:
391:    `max_workers` caps the global concurrency. Per-backend
392:    limits gate concurrent-by-backend further: a task on
393:    backend `"semantic_scholar"` can only proceed when both a
394:    worker is free AND the backend's semaphore has capacity.
395:
396:    `per_backend_max_concurrent` maps `backend_id` to its
397:    concurrency limit. Backends absent from the map use
398:    `DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT`. Pass `{"semantic_scholar": 1,
399:    "serper": 10, ...}` to set explicit ceilings.
400:
401:    `per_task_timeout` is the wall-clock seconds before a
402:    task is marked `TIMEOUT`. None disables timeout.
403:    The cancel attempt is best-effort: in-flight tasks may
404:    continue running after the timeout fires (Python's
405:    Future.cancel() can't interrupt synchronous code), but
406:    the report records TIMEOUT and the worker thread is
407:    abandoned. Backend semaphore is released either way (in a
408:    finally block), so subsequent tasks on the same backend
409:    don't deadlock waiting on a leaked permit.
410:
411:    Returns a `ParallelFetchReport` summarizing per-task
412:    outcomes. Order of `results` is the input task order,
413:    NOT completion order — callers correlate via task_metadata
414:    if they need other orderings.
415:
416:    Duplicate (source_url, backend_id) pairs in input collapse
417:    to first-occurrence (subsequent dupes dropped silently).
418:    """
419:    if not isinstance(tasks, Sequence) or isinstance(
420:        tasks, (str, bytes)
421:    ):
422:        raise ParallelFetchError(
423:            f"tasks must be a sequence of FetchTask, got "
424:            f"{type(tasks).__name__}"
425:        )
426:    if fetcher is None or not hasattr(fetcher, "fetch"):
427:        raise ParallelFetchError(
428:            "fetcher must implement the ParallelFetcher Protocol"
429:        )
430:    if max_workers < 1:
431:        raise ParallelFetchError(
432:            f"max_workers must be >= 1, got {max_workers}"
433:        )
434:    if per_task_timeout is not None and per_task_timeout <= 0:
435:        raise ParallelFetchError(
436:            f"per_task_timeout must be > 0 or None, got "
437:            f"{per_task_timeout}"
438:        )
439:
440:    if per_backend_max_concurrent is not None:
441:        for k, v in per_backend_max_concurrent.items():
442:            if not isinstance(v, int) or v < 1:
443:                raise ParallelFetchError(
444:                    f"per_backend_max_concurrent[{k!r}] must be "
445:                    f"a positive int, got {v!r}"
446:                )
447:
448:    # Build deduplicated task list (first wins).
449:    seen_keys: set[tuple[str, str]] = set()
450:    deduped: list[FetchTask] = []
451:    for i, task in enumerate(tasks):
452:        if not isinstance(task, FetchTask):
453:            raise ParallelFetchError(
454:                f"tasks[{i}] must be FetchTask, got "
455:                f"{type(task).__name__}"
456:            )
457:        key = (task.source_url, task.backend_id)
458:        if key in seen_keys:
459:            continue
460:        seen_keys.add(key)
461:        deduped.append(task)
462:
463:    started_at = time.time()
464:
465:    if not deduped:
466:        return ParallelFetchReport(
467:            started_at=started_at,
468:            finished_at=started_at,
469:            results=(),
470:            success_count=0,
471:            errored_count=0,
472:            timeout_count=0,
473:        )
474:
475:    # Per-backend semaphores. Lazily allocated on first task
476:    # for each backend.
477:    backend_limits: dict[str, int] = dict(per_backend_max_concurrent or {})
478:    semaphores: dict[str, threading.Semaphore] = {}
479:    semaphores_lock = threading.Lock()
480:
481:    def _get_semaphore(backend_id: str) -> threading.Semaphore:
482:        with semaphores_lock:
483:            sem = semaphores.get(backend_id)
484:            if sem is None:
485:                limit = backend_limits.get(
486:                    backend_id, DEFAULT_PER_BACKEND_LIMIT,
487:                )
488:                sem = threading.Semaphore(limit)
489:                semaphores[backend_id] = sem
490:            return sem
491:
492:    # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): the deadline is START-anchored, so we record the
493:    # MONOTONIC start of each task (immune to wall-clock jumps) keyed by index
494:    # under the lock. The wall-clock start is kept separately ONLY for the
495:    # FetchResultRecord.started_at report field (AC7 — never used in deadline
496:    # arithmetic). A not-yet-started index is absent from BOTH maps → +inf
497:    # effective deadline → cannot TIMEOUT before it runs.
498:    task_started_monotonic_by_index: dict[int, float] = {}
499:    task_started_wall_by_index: dict[int, float] = {}
500:    task_started_lock = threading.Lock()
501:
502:    def _run_task(
503:        index: int, task: FetchTask,
504:    ) -> FetchResultRecord:
505:        sem = _get_semaphore(task.backend_id)
506:        sem.acquire()
507:        task_started = time.time()
508:        task_started_mono = time.monotonic()
509:        with task_started_lock:
510:            task_started_monotonic_by_index[index] = task_started_mono
511:            task_started_wall_by_index[index] = task_started
512:        try:
513:            try:
514:                result = fetcher.fetch(task)
515:            except Exception as exc:  # noqa: BLE001 — intentional broad
516:                return FetchResultRecord(
517:                    source_url=task.source_url,
518:                    backend_id=task.backend_id,
519:                    outcome=FetchOutcome.ERRORED,
520:                    payload=None,
521:                    content_type=None,
522:                    fetch_status_code=None,
523:                    error=str(exc),
524:                    started_at=task_started,
525:                    finished_at=time.time(),
526:                    task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
527:                )
528:
529:            if (
530:                not isinstance(result, tuple)
531:                or len(result) != 3
532:                or not isinstance(result[0], (bytes, bytearray))
533:                or not isinstance(result[1], str)
534:                or not isinstance(result[2], int)
535:            ):
536:                raise FetcherProtocolError(
537:                    f"fetcher.fetch({task.source_url!r}) returned "
538:                    f"{type(result).__name__}, expected "
539:                    "tuple[bytes, str, int]"
540:                )
541:
542:            payload, content_type, status = result
543:            return FetchResultRecord(
544:                source_url=task.source_url,
545:                backend_id=task.backend_id,
546:                outcome=FetchOutcome.SUCCESS,
547:                payload=bytes(payload),
548:                content_type=content_type,
549:                fetch_status_code=status,
550:                error=None,
551:                started_at=task_started,
552:                finished_at=time.time(),
553:                task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
554:            )
555:        finally:
556:            sem.release()
557:
558:    results_by_index: dict[int, FetchResultRecord] = {}
559:
560:    # Codex round-1 HIGH fix (v2): manage executor manually so we

exec
"C:\\Program Files\\PowerShell\\7\\pwsh.exe" -Command '$path='"'src\\polaris_graph\\audit_ir\\parallel_fetch.py'; "'$start=560; $end=810; $i=0; Get-Content $path | ForEach-Object { $i++; if($i -ge $start -and $i -le $end){ '"'{0}:{1}' -f "'$i,$_ } }' in C:\POLARIS
 succeeded in 672ms:
560:    # Codex round-1 HIGH fix (v2): manage executor manually so we
561:    # can shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) on TIMEOUT.
562:    # The `with` block's __exit__ calls shutdown(wait=True) which
563:    # blocks until all in-flight workers finish — defeating
564:    # boundary 3's caller-latency promise.
565:    executor = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(
566:        max_workers=max_workers,
567:    )
568:    try:
569:        future_to_index: dict[concurrent.futures.Future, int] = {}
570:        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C02): submit in ROUND-ROBIN backend order so the
571:        # first `max_workers` submissions span up to `max_workers` distinct
572:        # backends/hosts. Without this, a clustered same-host candidate prefix
573:        # makes the first workers all block on ONE host-semaphore (acquired
574:        # inside `_run_task`), starving distinct-host tasks queued behind them.
575:        # `_round_robin_indices` returns ORIGINAL deduped indices, so result
576:        # indexing into `results_by_index` is unchanged (correctness preserved).
577:        submit_order = _round_robin_indices(deduped)
578:        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): anchor the GLOBAL batch budget at submit.
579:        batch_start = time.monotonic()
580:        for idx in submit_order:
581:            task = deduped[idx]
582:            fut = executor.submit(_run_task, idx, task)
583:            future_to_index[fut] = idx
584:
585:        # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): derive the GLOBAL batch budget — the
586:        # termination backstop that guarantees this call returns in bounded
587:        # time even when every worker is wedged on an abandoned in-flight fetch
588:        # and the queued siblings keep their +inf per-task deadline. Env wins;
589:        # else derive from per_task_timeout and the wave count; else (no
590:        # per-task timeout AND no env) the batch budget is disabled (None),
591:        # preserving the existing "None disables timeout" contract.
592:        batch_budget = _derive_batch_budget_seconds(
593:            num_tasks=len(deduped),
594:            max_workers=max_workers,
595:            per_task_timeout=per_task_timeout,
596:        )
597:
598:        remaining: set[concurrent.futures.Future] = set(future_to_index)
599:        protocol_error_to_raise: FetcherProtocolError | None = None
600:
601:        def _effective_deadline_monotonic(idx: int) -> float:
602:            # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): START-anchored, MONOTONIC effective
603:            # deadline. A not-yet-started index (absent from the start map)
604:            # → +inf: it CANNOT TIMEOUT before it runs. A started index →
605:            # its OWN observed start + per_task_timeout. Caller holds
606:            # `task_started_lock`.
607:            started = task_started_monotonic_by_index.get(idx)
608:            if started is None:
609:                return float("inf")
610:            return started + per_task_timeout  # type: ignore[operator]
611:
612:        batch_deadline = (
613:            batch_start + batch_budget if batch_budget is not None else None
614:        )
615:
616:        while remaining and protocol_error_to_raise is None:
617:            if per_task_timeout is None and batch_deadline is None:
618:                # No per-task timeout AND no batch budget — just wait for the
619:                # next completion (the original "None disables timeout" path).
620:                done, _ = concurrent.futures.wait(
621:                    remaining,
622:                    return_when=concurrent.futures.FIRST_COMPLETED,
623:                )
624:            else:
625:                # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01): BOUNDED-POLL harvest. The wait is
626:                # ALWAYS bounded by `_HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS`, never
627:                # `timeout=None`, so a task that STARTS after this snapshot
628:                # (or one that hangs), AND the GLOBAL batch deadline, are
629:                # re-checked within the poll interval — the harvest never
630:                # relies on a sibling completing to make progress. When there
631:                # is no per-task timeout (batch budget only), the per-task
632:                # deadlines are all +inf so the poll interval alone bounds the
633:                # wait until the batch deadline fires.
634:                now_mono = time.monotonic()
635:                if per_task_timeout is None:
636:                    next_deadline = float("inf")
637:                else:
638:                    with task_started_lock:
639:                        next_deadline = min(
640:                            _effective_deadline_monotonic(future_to_index[f])
641:                            for f in remaining
642:                        )
643:                if batch_deadline is not None:
644:                    next_deadline = min(next_deadline, batch_deadline)
645:                wait_timeout = min(
646:                    max(0.0, next_deadline - now_mono),
647:                    _HARVEST_POLL_INTERVAL_SECONDS,
648:                )
649:                done, _ = concurrent.futures.wait(
650:                    remaining,
651:                    timeout=wait_timeout,
652:                    return_when=concurrent.futures.FIRST_COMPLETED,
653:                )
654:
655:            if done:
656:                for fut in done:
657:                    idx = future_to_index[fut]
658:                    task = deduped[idx]
659:                    try:
660:                        rec = fut.result()
661:                    except FetcherProtocolError as exc:
662:                        protocol_error_to_raise = exc
663:                        break
664:                    results_by_index[idx] = rec
665:                    remaining.discard(fut)
666:
667:            if protocol_error_to_raise is not None:
668:                break
669:
670:            if per_task_timeout is not None:
671:                # Recompute the expired set from CURRENT start-times after
672:                # every wake (whether or not anything completed). A task is
673:                # TIMEOUT iff IT exceeded ITS OWN start-anchored budget.
674:                now_mono = time.monotonic()
675:                with task_started_lock:
676:                    expired = [
677:                        f for f in list(remaining)
678:                        if _effective_deadline_monotonic(
679:                            future_to_index[f]
680:                        ) <= now_mono
681:                    ]
682:                if expired:
683:                    now_wall = time.time()
684:                    for fut in expired:
685:                        idx = future_to_index[fut]
686:                        task = deduped[idx]
687:                        fut.cancel()  # best-effort
688:                        with task_started_lock:
689:                            timeout_started_at = (
690:                                task_started_wall_by_index.get(idx, now_wall)
691:                            )
692:                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
693:                            source_url=task.source_url,
694:                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
695:                            outcome=FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT,
696:                            payload=None,
697:                            content_type=None,
698:                            fetch_status_code=None,
699:                            error="per-task timeout exceeded",
700:                            started_at=timeout_started_at,
701:                            finished_at=now_wall,
702:                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
703:                        )
704:                        remaining.discard(fut)
705:
706:            # I-fetch-003 (BB5-C01 P1): GLOBAL batch-budget termination
707:            # guarantee. If the batch deadline has passed, the batch did NOT
708:            # converge in bounded time — almost always because every worker is
709:            # wedged on an abandoned (timed-out but un-cancellable) in-flight
710:            # fetch while distinct-host siblings sit queued behind it forever.
711:            # Record EVERY still-remaining future (not just never-started ones,
712:            # or the final `results_by_index[i]` reduction KeyErrors on a
713:            # started-but-unfinished index) and break so the call ALWAYS
714:            # returns:
715:            #   - started-but-unfinished (idx in the start map) → TIMEOUT (it
716:            #     ran and over-ran; same class as a normal per-task timeout).
717:            #   - never-started (idx absent from the start map) → NOT_DISPATCHED
718:            #     (it never reached the fetcher; distinct outcome, NOT a
719:            #     mislabelled TIMEOUT).
720:            if (
721:                batch_deadline is not None
722:                and remaining
723:                and time.monotonic() > batch_deadline
724:            ):
725:                now_wall = time.time()
726:                for fut in list(remaining):
727:                    idx = future_to_index[fut]
728:                    task = deduped[idx]
729:                    fut.cancel()  # best-effort
730:                    with task_started_lock:
731:                        was_started = (
732:                            idx in task_started_monotonic_by_index
733:                        )
734:                        started_wall = task_started_wall_by_index.get(
735:                            idx, now_wall,
736:                        )
737:                    if was_started:
738:                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
739:                            source_url=task.source_url,
740:                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
741:                            outcome=FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT,
742:                            payload=None,
743:                            content_type=None,
744:                            fetch_status_code=None,
745:                            error="per-task timeout exceeded",
746:                            started_at=started_wall,
747:                            finished_at=now_wall,
748:                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
749:                        )
750:                    else:
751:                        results_by_index[idx] = FetchResultRecord(
752:                            source_url=task.source_url,
753:                            backend_id=task.backend_id,
754:                            outcome=FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED,
755:                            payload=None,
756:                            content_type=None,
757:                            fetch_status_code=None,
758:                            error=(
759:                                "never dispatched: parallel-fetch batch "
760:                                "budget exceeded (worker pool starved by "
761:                                "abandoned in-flight tasks)"
762:                            ),
763:                            started_at=now_wall,
764:                            finished_at=now_wall,
765:                            task_metadata=task.task_metadata,
766:                        )
767:                    remaining.discard(fut)
768:                break
769:
770:        if protocol_error_to_raise is not None:
771:            for other_fut in remaining:
772:                other_fut.cancel()
773:            # Codex round-1 MEDIUM fix (v2): release the executor
774:            # without waiting so the protocol error propagates
775:            # promptly. In-flight workers continue but caller
776:            # gets control back.
777:            executor.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
778:            raise protocol_error_to_raise
779:    finally:
780:        # Codex round-1 HIGH fix (v2): non-blocking shutdown.
781:        # Pending (not-yet-started) futures are cancelled;
782:        # in-flight workers continue but don't gate our return.
783:        executor.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
784:
785:    # Reorder by input index.
786:    results = tuple(
787:        results_by_index[i] for i in range(len(deduped))
788:    )
789:
790:    success = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.SUCCESS)
791:    errored = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.ERRORED)
792:    timed_out = sum(1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.TIMEOUT)
793:    not_dispatched = sum(
794:        1 for r in results if r.outcome == FetchOutcome.NOT_DISPATCHED
795:    )
796:
797:    return ParallelFetchReport(
798:        started_at=started_at,
799:        finished_at=time.time(),
800:        results=results,
801:        success_count=success,
802:        errored_count=errored,
803:        timeout_count=timed_out,
804:        not_dispatched_count=not_dispatched,
805:    )

1:"""Sweep-side 4-role evaluation orchestration (I-meta-002 sub-PR-6).
2:
3:This module is the THIN seam between `scripts/run_honest_sweep_r3.py` and the per-claim
4:4-role pipeline (`role_pipeline.run_claim_pipeline`, sub-PR-5). It exists so the big sweep
5:file's edit stays minimal: the sweep supplies an INJECTED `RoleTransport` plus the already-
6:extracted per-claim inputs, and this module drives the Mirror -> Sentinel -> Judge pipeline
7:for each claim, feeds the resulting `D8ClaimRow`s through the SINGLE binding gate
8:(`apply_d8_release_policy`, sub-PR-3), persists the snowball KG (sub-PR-5), and returns a
9:flat result the sweep maps onto the manifest + `VerifiedSentence.evaluator_agrees`.
10:
11:The seam itself adds no paid LLM call; the dependency-injected transport performs the
12:(already-paid) verifier calls, and the per-call run-budget guard lives in
13:`role_pipeline.RecordingTransport` (I-meta-008 — verifier spend is threaded into the shared
14:PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN accumulator there, so a cap breach raises `BudgetExceededError` out of
15:this seam). Tests inject a mock transport. There is NO `datetime.now()`: the caller supplies
16:the audit `timestamp` (LAW VI). The KG opens a SQLite file under the caller-supplied `run_dir`
17:only — no other I/O except the D8 config YAML read (a pure file read).
18:
19:Fail-closed contract (Codex P2 directives, binding):
20:
21:  * D8 IS THE SINGLE BINDING GATE. `apply_d8_release_policy` produces the headline
22:    `release_allowed`. The legacy `evaluator_gate` may be emitted by the sweep as ADVISORY
23:    metadata only; this module never reads or writes it.
24:  * NO synthesized claim_ids. Each claim carries its existing `claim_id` (from the verified
25:    sentence / section). A blank id fails LOUD (`ValueError`) — duplicate/edited claims must
26:    not collide and break rewrite/gap traceability.
27:  * NO empty / vacuous D8 pass. An empty claim set OR an empty canonical required-element set
28:    (the coverage denominator) fails LOUD before any pipeline call — a `fraction()==1.0` over
29:    an empty denominator must never be allowed to release nothing as "all good".
30:  * Coverage denominator comes from the CANONICAL required elements (`required_element_ids`,
31:    caller-supplied), NOT from which claims happened to survive. `covered_element_ids` is
32:    populated ONLY from VERIFIED final verdicts via the caller-supplied claim->element map,
33:    so dropping/refusing a claim lowers coverage rather than dodging the gate.

74:# Canonical verdict token used to decide reuse / coverage credit (never an Enum here).
75:_VERDICT_VERIFIED = "VERIFIED"
76:
77:# The three role slots the per-claim pipeline expects in its `model_slugs` map.
78:_REQUIRED_ROLE_SLUG_KEYS = ("mirror", "sentinel", "judge")
79:
80:# I-run11-001 (#1042): bounded per-claim COMPUTE parallelism for the 4-role seam. The per-claim
81:# Mirror->Sentinel->Judge pipeline is independent across claims (each `run_claim_pipeline` builds
82:# its OWN `RecordingTransport`), so the COMPUTE half can run in a small thread pool while ALL
83:# reduction + persistence (D8 policy, coverage credit, KG write, run-budget cap) stays on the
84:# PARENT thread in ORIGINAL claim order (Codex Path-B SAFE design, .codex/I-run11-seam). At the
85:# benchmark stage (xhigh reasoning, minutes/claim) this is what makes the seam finish in time;
86:# run 10 died on the sequential operational failure mode. LAW VI: worker count from env only.
87:# `1` (or a single claim) preserves the EXACT sequential behaviour, including the live per-call
88:# budget enforcement inside `RecordingTransport.complete()`.
89:_CLAIM_WORKERS = max(1, int(os.getenv("PG_FOUR_ROLE_CLAIM_WORKERS", "6")))
90:
91:# I-run11-001 (#1042) Codex iter-2 P2.3: tiny on-disk progress marker the PARALLEL compute writes
92:# after EACH claim completes ({"done": k, "total": n}), so a hung mid-compute is visible on disk
93:# DURING compute — the role_call_log only grows during the later, parent-only reduction.
94:FOUR_ROLE_COMPUTE_PROGRESS_FILENAME = "four_role_compute_progress.json"

97:@dataclass
98:class FourRoleClaim:
99:    """One claim handed to the 4-role evaluation, carrying its EXISTING identity.
100:
101:    `claim_id` is the verified-sentence / section id already minted upstream — it is used
102:    verbatim for `D8ClaimRow.claim_id` and is NEVER synthesized here. `covered_element_ids`
103:    lists the CANONICAL required-element ids this claim would satisfy IF it clears the
104:    pipeline as VERIFIED; the ledger credits them only on a VERIFIED final verdict, so a
105:    dropped/UNSUPPORTED claim contributes nothing to coverage.
106:    """
107:
108:    claim_id: str
109:    claim_text: str
110:    evidence_documents: list[EvidenceDocument]
111:    severity: str
112:    s0_categories: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
113:    covered_element_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
114:
115:
116:@dataclass
117:class FourRoleEvaluationInputs:
118:    """Caller-supplied inputs for the GUARDED sweep 4-role branch (sub-PR-6).
119:
120:    Bundles everything the sweep hands to `run_four_role_evaluation` so the big sweep file's
121:    edit stays minimal and the contract is explicit: the per-claim `claims` (each with its
122:    EXISTING id), the canonical-denominator `coverage_ledger`, the per-question
123:    `required_s0_categories`, and the pinned `model_slugs` (mirror/sentinel/judge). The sweep
124:    NEVER builds these from the report — the caller (Gate-B) supplies them, so the sweep cannot
125:    synthesize a claim_id or a coverage denominator.
126:    """
127:
128:    claims: list[FourRoleClaim]
129:    coverage_ledger: CoverageLedger
130:    required_s0_categories: list[str]
131:    model_slugs: dict[str, str]
132:    rewrite_already_attempted: bool = False
133:
134:
135:@dataclass
136:class FourRoleEvaluationResult:
137:    """Flat result the sweep maps onto the manifest + per-sentence evaluator_agrees.
138:
139:    `release_allowed` / `held_reasons` / `gaps` come from the SINGLE binding D8 decision.
140:    `final_verdicts` maps claim_id -> final composed verdict (the sweep maps VERIFIED -> True
141:    and everything else -> False to populate `VerifiedSentence.evaluator_agrees`). `records`
142:    is the complete served-identity audit trail across all claims (for the Path-B identity
143:    gate). `coverage_fraction` is the canonical-denominator coverage actually achieved.
144:    """
145:
146:    release_allowed: bool
147:    held_reasons: list[str]
148:    gaps: list[Gap]
149:    final_verdicts: dict[str, str]
150:    records: list[RoleCallRecord]
151:    coverage_fraction: float
152:    fabricated_occurrence_latched: bool
153:    needs_rewrite: list[str]
154:    kg_path: Path
155:    # I-perm-001 (#1195) slice 2: the always-release-aware outcome (BLOCK->LABEL). Default None
156:    # for legacy/timeout construction sites; the headline D8 decision (release_allowed above) is
157:    # unchanged, so consumers that ignore this field are byte-identical.
158:    release_outcome: ReleaseOutcome | None = None
159:
160:
161:def evaluator_agrees_from_verdict(final_verdict: str) -> bool:
162:    """Map a composed final verdict to the two-family `evaluator_agrees` boolean.
163:
164:    VERIFIED -> True (the evaluator stack confirms the claim); every other verdict
165:    (PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED / UNREACHABLE) -> False. The sweep applies this ONLY
166:    to kept sentences (`verifier_pass=True`), so the `VerifiedSentence` validator that forbids
167:    `evaluator_agrees=True` on a dropped sentence is never tripped.
168:    """
169:    return final_verdict == _VERDICT_VERIFIED
170:
171:
172:def build_evaluator_agrees_map(
173:    final_verdicts: dict[str, str],
174:    kept_claim_ids: set[str] | None = None,
175:) -> dict[str, bool]:
176:    """Build the per-claim `evaluator_agrees` MAP for the sweep manifest (I-meta-002 PR-9/M5).
177:
178:    Maps each `claim_id` in `final_verdicts` to the §-1.1 fail-safe boolean:
179:
180:        evaluator_agrees = (claim is kept) AND evaluator_agrees_from_verdict(final_verdict)
181:
182:    where `evaluator_agrees_from_verdict` is the SINGLE source of the verdict rule
183:    (`final_verdict == "VERIFIED"`). Every other verdict — PARTIAL / UNSUPPORTED / FABRICATED /
184:    UNREACHABLE / any unknown string — maps to False. The value is NEVER True before a VERIFIED
185:    final verdict exists AND the claim is kept (a non-VERIFIED verdict must never read as
186:    "evaluator agreed" in clinical context).
187:
188:    `kept_claim_ids` is the set of claim_ids that survived as kept/verified sentences
189:    (`verifier_pass=True`). When it is None, ALL claim_ids in `final_verdicts` are treated as
190:    kept — INVARIANT: on the sweep path the `FourRoleClaim` set is built (by the M3a builder)
191:    from KEPT (is_verified) sentences only, so every claim_id in `final_verdicts` is already a
192:    kept claim and passing None is correct there. When a set IS supplied, a claim_id absent from
193:    it maps to False even if its verdict is VERIFIED (defensive: a not-kept claim can never agree).
194:
195:    Keys are EXACTLY `final_verdicts.keys()` (so the map is joinable to
196:    `four_role_claim_audit.json` from M3b); `kept_claim_ids` affects only the boolean value and
197:    never adds or removes keys. An empty `final_verdicts` yields `{}` (no error here; the sweep
198:    branch's own fail-closed guards already handle empty claim sets upstream).
199:    """
200:    agrees_map: dict[str, bool] = {}
201:    for claim_id, final_verdict in final_verdicts.items():
202:        is_kept = kept_claim_ids is None or claim_id in kept_claim_ids
203:        agrees_map[claim_id] = is_kept and evaluator_agrees_from_verdict(final_verdict)
204:    return agrees_map
205:
206:
207:def _write_role_call_log(path: Path, role_call_log: list[dict]) -> None:
208:    """Write the per-role-call reasoning log as one sorted-key JSON object per line.
209:
210:    I-run11-001 (#1042): factored out of `run_four_role_evaluation` so the SAME serialization is
211:    used by both the INCREMENTAL per-claim write (mid-run monitorability) and the final idempotent
212:    write. Byte-identical to the prior inline `write_text` (`ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True`),
213:    so a partial file rewritten on the next claim is just a longer prefix of the same content.
214:    """
215:    path.write_text(
216:        "".join(
217:            json.dumps(entry, ensure_ascii=False, sort_keys=True) + "\n"
218:            for entry in role_call_log
219:        ),
220:        encoding="utf-8",
221:    )
222:
223:
224:def _compute_claim_results(
225:    transport: RoleTransport,
226:    *,
227:    claims: list[FourRoleClaim],
228:    model_slugs: dict[str, str],
229:    timestamp: str,
230:    run_dir: Path,
231:    heartbeat_claims_cb=None,
232:) -> list[tuple[ClaimPipelineResult, float | None]]:
233:    """COMPUTE the per-claim 4-role pipeline for every claim, returning results BY INPUT INDEX.
234:
235:    I-run11-001 (#1042), Codex Path-B SAFE design (.codex/I-run11-seam). This is the ONLY half
236:    that runs the (already-paid) verifier calls; it makes NO reduction decision — the parent
237:    reduces in input order. Each element is `(ClaimPipelineResult, cost_delta)` for `claims[idx]`,
238:    where:
239:
240:      * SEQUENTIAL (`_CLAIM_WORKERS == 1` or a single claim): `run_claim_pipeline` is called
241:        DIRECTLY on the parent thread (no copied context, no `reset_run_cost`), so
242:        `RecordingTransport.complete()` enforces the LIVE per-call run budget on the parent
243:        `_RUN_COST_CTX` EXACTLY as today. `cost_delta is None` signals the parent "already
244:        accounted — do not re-add" (byte-equivalence with the pre-#1042 behaviour).
245:
246:      * PARALLEL: each claim runs inside its OWN `contextvars.copy_context()` snapshot that is taken
247:        ON THE PARENT THREAD BEFORE submit (Codex iter-2 P1.2 — copying inside the worker would
248:        snapshot the worker's EMPTY default context, so the parent Path-B capture sink
249:        `pathB_capture._SINK` / `_ROLE` registered by `pathB_runner` on the PARENT would be ABSENT
250:        and verifier capture would no-op, failing post-run completeness when workers>1). Each claim
251:        gets its OWN copy (never one shared copy across concurrent workers — a copy carries the
252:        worker's isolated `_RUN_COST_CTX` AND the parent's `_SINK` reference). The worker
253:        `reset_run_cost()`s its copy, runs the pipeline, and returns `current_run_cost()` as
254:        `cost_delta`; the PARENT re-adds it to the single run counter and enforces the cap DURING
255:        compute (Codex iter-2 P1.1) so overspend is bounded to the workers in-flight at breach
256:        (~workers-1), not all claims. The pathB capture `_SINK` is shared BY REFERENCE through the
257:        copied context (atomic `list.append`), so verifier captures land at the PARENT sink for the
258:        M4 gate — it is NOT isolated. A worker exception PROPAGATES (fail closed; no `except: pass`).
259:    """
260:    n = len(claims)
261:
262:    # I-ready-017 FX-08b (#1113): CLAIM-LEVEL DEDUP (BUG-04 determinism half).
263:    # 4+ byte-identical claims were SPLIT VERIFIED/UNSUPPORTED in the same run
264:    # (a non-deterministic release gate). Two claims whose ENTIRE pipeline INPUT
265:    # is identical MUST get the same verdict — so run the pipeline ONCE per
266:    # distinct input and fan the verdict out. The key is the FULL input the
267:    # pipeline consumes (claim_text + evidence doc_id+text + severity +
268:    # s0_categories), NOT just (sentence+ids+spans): anything that could
269:    # legitimately change the verdict is in the key, so two genuinely-different
270:    # claims can NEVER collapse to one verdict (faithfulness-safe; identical
271:    # input => identical output is the only justification for sharing a verdict).
272:    # The evidence doc `text` is the FX-03 cited WINDOW, so it encodes the span.
273:    # Fan-out is HONEST on the audit trail + cost: a duplicate gets its OWN
274:    # claim_id (dataclasses.replace on d8_row), the SHARED verdict + sub-results,
275:    # EMPTY records (no new model calls were made for it, so all_records /
276:    # role_call_log are not inflated), and zero cost (cost_delta None). Coverage
277:    # credit still flows per-claim (each dup's covered_element_ids on its VERIFIED
278:    # verdict). Flag-gated (PG_FOUR_ROLE_CLAIM_DEDUP, default ON); OFF reverts to
279:    # the per-claim run. Recursion on representatives is one level deep (reps have
280:    # all-distinct keys -> this branch is skipped on the inner call).
281:    _dedup_enabled = os.getenv("PG_FOUR_ROLE_CLAIM_DEDUP", "1") != "0"
282:
283:    def _dedup_key(c: FourRoleClaim) -> tuple:
284:        return (
285:            " ".join((c.claim_text or "").split()),
286:            tuple(sorted(
287:                (d.doc_id, d.text) for d in (c.evidence_documents or [])
288:            )),
289:            c.severity,
290:            tuple(sorted(c.s0_categories or [])),
291:        )
292:
293:    if _dedup_enabled and n > 1:
294:        rep_idx_by_key: dict[tuple, int] = {}
295:        rep_indices: list[int] = []
296:        for idx, claim in enumerate(claims):
297:            k = _dedup_key(claim)
298:            if k not in rep_idx_by_key:
299:                rep_idx_by_key[k] = idx
300:                rep_indices.append(idx)
301:        if len(rep_indices) < n:
302:            # At least one duplicate — run the pipeline only on representatives.
303:            rep_results = _compute_claim_results(
304:                transport,
305:                claims=[claims[i] for i in rep_indices],
306:                model_slugs=model_slugs,
307:                timestamp=timestamp,
308:                run_dir=run_dir,
309:                heartbeat_claims_cb=heartbeat_claims_cb,  # Codex AC1-gate P2-1: dedup path too
310:            )
311:            rep_result_by_idx = {
312:                rep_indices[j]: rep_results[j] for j in range(len(rep_indices))
313:            }
314:            fanned: list[tuple[ClaimPipelineResult, float | None]] = []
315:            for idx, claim in enumerate(claims):
316:                rep_idx = rep_idx_by_key[_dedup_key(claim)]
317:                rep_res, rep_cost = rep_result_by_idx[rep_idx]
318:                if idx == rep_idx:
319:                    fanned.append((rep_res, rep_cost))
320:                else:
321:                    # Fan the verdict out to this duplicate under ITS own
322:                    # claim_id; no new model calls (records=[]) and no new spend
323:                    # (cost None) — the representative already paid + recorded.
324:                    dup_row = replace(rep_res.d8_row, claim_id=claim.claim_id)
325:                    dup_res = replace(rep_res, d8_row=dup_row, records=[])
326:                    fanned.append((dup_res, None))
327:            return fanned
328:
329:    def _compute_one(
330:        idx: int, claim: FourRoleClaim, worker_ctx: contextvars.Context
331:    ) -> tuple[int, ClaimPipelineResult, float]:
332:        def _run() -> tuple[ClaimPipelineResult, float]:
333:            # Isolate THIS claim's verifier spend in the copied context so the parent can re-add a
334:            # clean per-claim delta and enforce the cap deterministically at the claim boundary.
335:            # `reset_run_cost()` zeroes ONLY this copy's `_RUN_COST_CTX`; the parent's `_SINK` /
336:            # `_ROLE` references carried by the same copy are untouched, so verifier capture lands
337:            # at the parent sink.
338:            reset_run_cost()
339:            res = run_claim_pipeline(
340:                transport,
341:                claim_id=claim.claim_id,
342:                claim=claim.claim_text,
343:                evidence_documents=claim.evidence_documents,
344:                severity=claim.severity,
345:                s0_categories=claim.s0_categories,
346:                model_slugs=model_slugs,
347:                timestamp=timestamp,
348:            )
349:            return res, current_run_cost()
350:
351:        # Run inside the PARENT-captured context snapshot (P1.2): the worker executes `_run` under
352:        # the parent's Path-B capture state, not the worker thread's empty default context.
353:        result, delta = worker_ctx.run(_run)
354:        return idx, result, delta
355:
356:    # SEQUENTIAL fast path: byte-equivalent to the pre-#1042 loop (live per-call budget preserved,
357:    # cost_delta None so the parent does not re-account). A single claim also takes this path (a
358:    # thread pool for one item is pure overhead).
359:    if _CLAIM_WORKERS == 1 or n <= 1:
360:        out: list[tuple[ClaimPipelineResult, float | None]] = []
361:        for claim in claims:
362:            result = run_claim_pipeline(
363:                transport,
364:                claim_id=claim.claim_id,
365:                claim=claim.claim_text,
366:                evidence_documents=claim.evidence_documents,
367:                severity=claim.severity,
368:                s0_categories=claim.s0_categories,
369:                model_slugs=model_slugs,
370:                timestamp=timestamp,
371:            )
372:            out.append((result, None))
373:        return out
374:
375:    # PARALLEL path (Codex iter-2 P1.1 + P1.2): enforce the run budget DURING compute, not after the
376:    # whole pool drains. Each claim is submitted with its OWN parent-captured context snapshot so the
377:    # worker inherits the Path-B capture sink/role registered on the parent. We iterate
378:    # `as_completed` and, for each completed future, re-add the worker's per-claim verifier delta to
379:    # the SINGLE parent run counter and re-check the cap — so a cumulative cap breach raises after
380:    # only ~(workers-in-flight) claims have spent, NOT all n. The pool is managed MANUALLY (not via
381:    # `with`, whose `__exit__` waits for ALL pending futures) so on a breach / worker exception we
382:    # `shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)` to cancel still-queued claims, then re-raise
383:    # (P2.2 cancel-on-fail).
384:    #
385:    # P2.1 (aborted-run cost under-accounting — documented, not over-engineered): on a worker
386:    # exception the worker's partial paid spend lives in the worker's isolated copied context and is
387:    # NOT reconciled into the parent counter. This is the SAME accepted tradeoff the seam-timeout
388:    # wrapper documents (run_honest_sweep_r3.py ~L4587-4596: in-flight verifier cost on the
389:    # held/aborted path is not reconciled) — prompt fail-closed termination outranks exact accounting
390:    # on an already-aborted run, and the operator authorized the spend.
391:    computed: list[tuple[ClaimPipelineResult, float | None] | None] = [None] * n
392:    # Codex iter-2 P1 (REGRESSION FIX): the progress write below targets `run_dir`, but
393:    # `_compute_claim_results` runs BEFORE `VerifiedClaimGraphStore(run_dir=...)` (which is what
394:    # historically created `run_dir`). Existing callers (scripts/dr_benchmark/offline_e2e.py,
395:    # tests/dr_benchmark/test_offline_e2e.py) pass a `run_dir` that does NOT yet exist, so the
396:    # parallel path's progress write raised FileNotFoundError. Ensure the dir exists exactly once
397:    # at the TOP of the parallel branch (idempotent; the later kg_store reuses the same dir —
398:    # harmless). The SEQUENTIAL fast path does NOT mkdir (it writes no progress file) so it stays
399:    # byte-equivalent to the pre-#1042 behaviour.
400:    run_dir.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
401:    progress_path = run_dir / FOUR_ROLE_COMPUTE_PROGRESS_FILENAME
402:    done = 0
403:    pool = concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=_CLAIM_WORKERS)
404:    try:
405:        futures = [
406:            pool.submit(_compute_one, idx, claim, contextvars.copy_context())
407:            for idx, claim in enumerate(claims)
408:        ]
409:        for future in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures):
410:            # `future.result()` re-raises any worker exception (fail closed) — handled below.
411:            idx, result, delta = future.result()
412:            computed[idx] = (result, delta)
413:            # P2.3 + Codex iter-2 P2 (observability): write the tiny on-disk progress marker BEFORE
414:            # the budget enforcement below, so the just-completed claim — INCLUDING a cap-breaching
415:            # one — is reflected in four_role_compute_progress.json even when the very next
416:            # check_run_budget(0) raises BudgetExceededError. `done` counts completed claims
417:            # including the current one. Parent-only write — never inside a worker. A hung compute
418:            # is thus visible on disk DURING compute (the role_call_log only grows during the later,
419:            # parent-only reduction).
420:            done += 1
421:            progress_path.write_text(
422:                json.dumps({"done": done, "total": n}, sort_keys=True) + "\n",
423:                encoding="utf-8",
424:            )
425:            # Enforce the run budget DURING compute (P1.1): thread this claim's verifier spend into
426:            # the SINGLE parent run counter and re-check the cap immediately. A `BudgetExceededError`
427:            # here bounds overspend to the workers in-flight at the breach (~workers-1), not all n.
428:            if delta is not None:
429:                _add_run_cost(delta)
430:                check_run_budget(0)  # raises BudgetExceededError if the cap is now exceeded.
431:            # I-obs-001 #1141 AC1 (Codex AC1-gate P2): forward done/total to the run-status heartbeat
432:            # AFTER _add_run_cost so running_cost_usd reflects the just-completed claim. The progress
433:            # marker above already recorded this claim BEFORE the budget check (cap-breach visibility);
434:            # if check_run_budget raised, the terminal heartbeat captures the abort. Opaque callback —
435:            # sweep_integration stays ignorant of the heartbeat schema; additive, never raises.
436:            if heartbeat_claims_cb is not None:
437:                try:
438:                    heartbeat_claims_cb(done, n)
439:                except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001
440:                    pass
441:    except BaseException:
442:        # On ANY failure (BudgetExceededError from the cap OR a propagated worker exception) cancel
443:        # still-pending claims and tear the pool down NON-BLOCKING (P2.2), then re-raise so the
444:        # existing budget-abort / fail-closed path in run_four_role_evaluation handles it. No
445:        # `except: pass` — the error always propagates.
446:        pool.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
447:        raise
448:    else:
449:        pool.shutdown(wait=True)
450:
451:    # Defensive: every index must be filled (a None here would mean a future silently dropped — a
452:    # real bug, never a vacuous pass). Fail loud rather than reduce a partial result set.
453:    for idx, item in enumerate(computed):
454:        if item is None:
455:            raise RuntimeError(
456:                f"run_four_role_evaluation: claim index {idx} produced no result from the "
457:                f"compute pool (fail-closed — a dropped future must never reduce silently)."
458:            )
459:    return [item for item in computed if item is not None]
460:
461:
462:def run_four_role_evaluation(
463:    transport: RoleTransport,
464:    *,
465:    claims: list[FourRoleClaim],
466:    run_dir: Path,
467:    timestamp: str,
468:    coverage_ledger: CoverageLedger,
469:    required_s0_categories: list[str],
470:    model_slugs: dict[str, str],
471:    rewrite_already_attempted: bool = False,
472:    d8_config_path: str | Path | None = None,
473:    campaign_kg_db: str | Path | None = None,
474:    heartbeat_claims_cb=None,
475:) -> FourRoleEvaluationResult:
476:    """Drive the per-claim 4-role pipeline, apply the SINGLE binding D8 gate, persist the KG.
477:
478:    I-meta-002-q1d (#948): when `campaign_kg_db` is given, the snowball KG is persisted to that
479:    CAMPAIGN-scoped sqlite path (shared across the sweep's questions) instead of the per-question
480:    `run_dir`, so question N can reuse questions 1..N-1's VERIFIED claims. Default None preserves the
481:    per-`run_dir` behavior (existing callers/tests unchanged). The sweep runs questions sequentially, so
482:    there are no concurrent writers; anti-poisoning (only VERIFIED rows reusable) is unchanged.
483:
484:    Pure orchestration over the INJECTED `transport` (no network, no spend). For EACH claim:
485:    run `run_claim_pipeline`, collect its `D8ClaimRow` + served-identity records + final
486:    verdict, and — only on a VERIFIED final verdict — credit that claim's canonical
487:    `covered_element_ids` into the ledger. Then run `apply_d8_release_policy` ONCE over the
488:    collected rows (D8 is the headline gate) and write every per-claim outcome to the snowball
489:    `VerifiedClaimGraphStore` under `run_dir`.
490:
491:    Fail-closed guards (Codex P2): raises `ValueError` on an empty claim set, an empty
492:    canonical required-element denominator, a blank/duplicate `claim_id`, or a missing role
493:    slug — never a vacuous pass and never a synthesized id.
494:    """
495:    # (P2) NO vacuous pass: an empty claim set or an empty canonical coverage denominator must
496:    # fail LOUD here, before any D8 call — fraction()==1.0 over an empty required set would
497:    # otherwise release "nothing" as all-clear.
498:    if not claims:
499:        raise ValueError(
500:            "run_four_role_evaluation: no claims supplied; a 4-role evaluation over zero "
501:            "claims cannot produce a release decision (fail-closed, no vacuous pass)."
502:        )
503:    if not coverage_ledger.required_element_ids:
504:        raise ValueError(
505:            "run_four_role_evaluation: coverage_ledger.required_element_ids is empty; the "
506:            "coverage denominator MUST be the canonical per-question required-element set "
507:            "(fail-closed, no vacuous pass over an empty denominator)."
508:        )
509:    # (Codex sub-PR-6 diff P1, LETHAL safety invariant) The coverage NUMERATOR must be rebuilt
510:    # here SOLELY from VERIFIED final verdicts — never trusted from the caller. A prefilled
511:    # `covered_element_ids` would let a Sentinel-UNGROUNDED claim that we correctly downgrade to
512:    # UNSUPPORTED still ride pre-credited coverage and release. Reject a non-empty incoming set
513:    # (fail LOUD), then credit a FRESH internal ledger only on VERIFIED finals below.
514:    if coverage_ledger.covered_element_ids:
515:        raise ValueError(
516:            "run_four_role_evaluation: coverage_ledger.covered_element_ids must be EMPTY on "
517:            "input; the coverage numerator is rebuilt here from VERIFIED final verdicts only, so "
518:            "a prefilled numerator cannot let an ungrounded/downgraded claim ride pre-credited "
519:            "coverage (fail-closed)."
520:        )

 succeeded in 1085ms:
260:        )
261:    return active_family

270:# Reasoning REQUEST param for MAX reasoning (operator directive: MAX reasoning at the benchmark

272:# (openrouter_client.py:1424): `{"effort": reasoning_effort, "enabled": True}`. OpenRouter's
273:# constraint is that `effort` and `max_tokens` are mutually exclusive; "xhigh" is OpenRouter's

276:# directive). Source: https://openrouter.ai/docs/guides/best-practices/reasoning-tokens .

281:# MiniMax-M2 call used reasoning + max_tokens>=3000; anything below that truncates the JSON
282:# {verdict, atoms} mid-emission (the run-12 truncator) and collapses every claim to a fail-closed
283:# UNGROUNDED. An env override (PG_SENTINEL_DECOMPOSITION_MAX_TOKENS) can RAISE but never lower past

287:# I-arch-003 (#1253) hardening (Codex gate P2): per-role CHAIN-MIN CEILING on the resolved total
288:# max_tokens (and the Mirror numeric reasoning cap). Under provider allow_fallbacks:false the binding
289:# output cap is the MIN max_completion_tokens across the role's pinned provider chain (live OpenRouter
290:# /endpoints read 2026-06-14). A budget that EXCEEDS this — whether the default or a future bad env
291:# override — would hard-400 ("requested N > max M") on the chain; that is exactly the failure class this
292:# audit closes. So every resolved budget is clamped DOWN to its chain min (env can lower for cost/testing
293:# but never raise past the provider cap). RE-DERIVE these if a role's chain in
294:# config/settings/openrouter_provider_routing.yaml is re-pinned to higher-cap-only providers.
295:_MIRROR_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 131072    # glm-5.1: atlas-cloud 202752, z-ai/baidu/novita/gmicloud >= 131072
296:_SENTINEL_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 131072  # minimax-m2: google/atlas-cloud 196608, novita/minimax 131072
297:_JUDGE_MAX_TOKENS_CHAIN_MIN = 262140     # qwen3.6: wandb 262144, io-net 262140

299:# I-meta-008 / #1026: blank-verdict step-down ladder. When a reasoning-first verifier returns a
300:# BLANK bare verdict (reasoning budget exhausted without converging under the high effort), retry
301:# with the effort stepped DOWN this ladder so the model is forced to spend tokens on the VERDICT,
302:# not endless reasoning. The first entry is the configured MAX effort; `None` means reasoning
303:# DISABLED (final resort — guarantees content). Overridable via PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER
304:# (comma-separated; the literal "off"/"none" -> reasoning disabled). MAX-reasoning-first, then a
305:# guaranteed verdict, then fail-loud only if even reasoning-off blanks.
306:def _parse_effort_ladder() -> tuple[object, ...]:
307:    raw = os.getenv("PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER")
308:    if not raw:
309:        return (_REASONING_EFFORT, "low", None)
310:    out: list[object] = []
311:    for token in raw.split(","):
312:        t = token.strip()
313:        if not t:
314:            continue
315:        out.append(None if t.lower() in ("off", "none", "disabled") else t)
316:    return tuple(out) if out else (_REASONING_EFFORT, "low", None)
317:

319:_VERIFIER_EFFORT_LADDER = _parse_effort_ladder()

321:# Per-role reasoning policy (Codex iter-2 P1 — reasoning is NOT a one-size-fits-all switch).
322:#
323:# WHY per-role: Mirror (`z-ai/glm-5.1`) and Judge (`qwen/qwen3.6-35b-a3b`) are DELIBERATIVE
324:# verifiers — they weigh evidence and argue a verdict, so they get MAX reasoning effort
325:# (`xhigh`) AND `provider.require_parameters=True` (only route to a provider that honors
326:# reasoning).
327:#
328:# Sentinel is MODE-AWARE (I-run11-004): the CERTIFIED MiniMax-M2 DECOMPOSITION Sentinel
329:# (`minimax/minimax-m2`, the benchmark + lock default) was certified WITH reasoning ON and
330:# max_tokens>=3000 — turning reasoning OFF (or starving max_tokens to the 256 classifier budget)
331:# truncates the JSON {verdict, atoms} mid-emission and collapses every claim to a fail-closed
332:# UNGROUNDED (the run-12 truncator). So when the active Sentinel groundedness mode is
333:# "decomposition", the Sentinel sends MAX reasoning + a GENEROUS max_tokens, exactly replicating
334:# the certified call. The SOVEREIGN `guardian`/`noninverted` granite Sentinel modes stay
335:# reasoning-OFF (a <score>/one-word classifier whose self-host slug does not advertise
336:# `reasoning`); their settings are NOT regressed. LAW VI: overridable via `PG_SENTINEL_REASONING`.
337:# I-run11-008 (#1053): Mirror keeps reasoning ON (bake-off: reasoning-OFF DOUBLED false_bind 1->2/5,
338:# i.e. a worse grounding judge), but its reasoning is BOUNDED numerically below (effort=xhigh is a
339:# no-op on GLM, so unbounded thinking exhausted max_tokens → the 47 blanks). Overridable via PG_MIRROR_REASONING.

343:def sentinel_decomposition_active(slug_override: str | None = None) -> bool:
344:    """True iff the ACTIVE Sentinel groundedness mode is "decomposition" (I-run11-004).
345:
346:    Lazily resolves `sentinel_adapter.sentinel_groundedness_mode()` (env- + model-aware, LAW VI)
347:    so the transport can mode-gate the Sentinel's reasoning + max_tokens WITHOUT an import-time
348:    dependency on the adapter (the adapter does not import this transport — no cycle). Any
349:    resolution error is swallowed to the conservative non-decomposition answer (the certified
350:    MiniMax call is only requested when the mode UNAMBIGUOUSLY resolves to decomposition).
351:
352:    `slug_override` (Codex diff-gate iter-4 P2) is the slug ACTUALLY being served on this call —
353:    threaded through from the request so the reasoning/max_tokens gate keys on the real served
354:    model, not a stale `PG_SENTINEL_MODEL` env, in any direct/future call path.
355:    """
356:    try:
357:        from src.polaris_graph.roles.sentinel_adapter import (
358:            _MODE_DECOMPOSITION,
359:            sentinel_groundedness_mode,
360:        )
361:    except (ImportError, AttributeError):
362:        # I-run11-010 (#1056, D4): only a genuine lazy-IMPORT failure (module missing / symbol
363:        # renamed) is safe to swallow as "not decomposition". A ValueError from
364:        # sentinel_groundedness_mode (an UNRECOGNIZED PG_SENTINEL_GROUNDEDNESS_MODE) is a caller
365:        # config bug and must NOT be silently downgraded to the 256-token classifier path (that
366:        # truncates the certified MiniMax-M2 JSON -> the run-12 all-UNGROUNDED collapse). It now
367:        # propagates (LAW II fail-loud) instead of being swallowed by a broad `except Exception`.
368:        return False
369:    return sentinel_groundedness_mode(slug_override) == _MODE_DECOMPOSITION

372:def role_reasoning_enabled(role: str, slug_override: str | None = None) -> bool:
373:    """Whether the given verifier role should send the MAX-reasoning request params.
374:
375:    Returns the per-role default from `_ROLE_REASONING_DEFAULT`, overridable per role via the
376:    env var `PG_<ROLE>_REASONING` (LAW VI): "1"/"true"/"yes" -> True, "0"/"false"/"no" -> False;
377:    absent (or any other value) falls back to the role default. Mirror/Judge default True
378:    (deliberative verifiers -> MAX reasoning). Sentinel is MODE-AWARE (I-run11-004): in
379:    "decomposition" mode (the certified MiniMax-M2 detector) it defaults True (reasoning ON, as
380:    certified); in the sovereign granite `guardian`/`noninverted` modes it defaults False (a
381:    classifier whose slug does not advertise `reasoning`, so reasoning would break routing).
382:    """
383:    if role == "sentinel" and os.getenv(f"PG_{role.upper()}_REASONING") is None:
384:        # No explicit override: the Sentinel default is mode-aware (decomposition -> reasoning ON),
385:        # keyed on the ACTUAL served slug when supplied (Codex diff-gate iter-4 P2).
386:        default = sentinel_decomposition_active(slug_override)
387:    else:
388:        default = _ROLE_REASONING_DEFAULT.get(role, False)
389:    override = os.getenv(f"PG_{role.upper()}_REASONING")
390:    if override is None:
391:        return default
392:    token = override.strip().lower()
393:    if token in ("1", "true", "yes"):
394:        return True
395:    if token in ("0", "false", "no"):
396:        return False
397:    return default

400:# Reuse the same timeout knob the self-host transport + openrouter_client use (LAW VI).
401:# I-meta-008 FULL-POWER: the reasoning verifiers (Mirror/Judge at effort=xhigh against a 16384-token
402:# budget) take MINUTES per claim — give them their OWN generous timeout (default 900s/15min), not the
403:# cheap 90s shared default (which also governs retrieval/embeddings). Env-overridable (LAW VI).
404:# NOTE: this is the PER-POST httpx timeout (bounds ONE network call). It does NOT bound the COMPOSED
405:# retry loop (effort-ladder x transport-retries x rate-limit-backoff), which can stack to hours per
406:# claim — that composition is bounded SEPARATELY by the #1226 wall-clock watchdog below.
407:_TIMEOUT_SECONDS = int(os.getenv("PG_VERIFIER_LLM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS", "900"))
408:
409:# === #1226 (I-pipe-001): blank-content bound + per-call wall-clock watchdog =====================
410:# THE FAILURE: a reasoning verifier (e.g. GLM-5.1 Mirror under xhigh) can burn its whole token
411:# budget on reasoning and emit EMPTY content every attempt; combined with the per-POST 900s timeout
412:# (_TIMEOUT_SECONDS) the COMPOSED retry loop (effort-ladder/provider-failover x transport-retries x
413:# rate-limit-backoff) can stall for HOURS per claim, so the D8 4-role gate never finishes and the
414:# whole sweep hangs. Two RELIABILITY backstops (NEVER touch a verify/NLI/4-role verdict; on
415:# exhaustion they re-raise the EXISTING fail-loud BlankVerdictError/RoleTransportError -> release
416:# stays HELD; no fake/empty verdict is ever synthesized):
417:#
418:#   (1) PG_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES (default 3): a HARD ceiling on the number of blank-content RETRIES.
419:#       Implemented by TRUNCATING the per-call effort/provider ladder to `1 + max_retries` attempts.
420:#       At the default (1 + 3 = 4) this ceiling is >= every role's existing default ladder length
421:#       (Judge 3, Mirror/routed-Sentinel 1+PG_PROVIDER_BLANK_RETRIES=4, classifier-Sentinel 1,
422:#       decomposition-Sentinel 3), so the truncation is the IDENTITY at default — it only bites when
423:#       a custom PG_PROVIDER_BLANK_RETRIES / PG_FOUR_ROLE_EFFORT_LADDER pushes a ladder past the cap.
424:#       Exhaustion fires the SAME existing `raise` at ladder-end -> the existing BlankVerdictError.
425:#
426:#   (2) PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S (default 3600s/1h): a COOPERATIVE wall-clock watchdog over the whole
427:#       `complete()` retry loop (checked at the top of each attempt + before each rate-limit sleep).
428:#       It does NOT interrupt an in-flight POST (httpx's _TIMEOUT_SECONDS bounds that) — it bounds the
429:#       COMPOSITION so a stuck-in-retries call aborts LOUDLY instead of hanging the sweep. 1h is a
430:#       deliberately-generous BACKSTOP against a wedged call, NOT a tight SLO: it sits well above any
431:#       HEALTHY 4-role call (a healthy claim resolves in seconds-to-minutes — the ladder rarely runs
432:#       to exhaustion, and a single blanking call returns its empty 200 quickly, it does not consume
433:#       the full per-POST 900s). The worst-case LEGITIMATE composition (every rung waiting near the
434:#       full _TIMEOUT_SECONDS, plus transport-retries and rate-limit backoff) CAN approach/exceed 1h;
435:#       that path is already pathological (it IS the stall this fix targets), so aborting it loudly is
436:#       the intended behavior, and the operator RAISES PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S if a slower legitimate
437:#       budget is ever needed (LAW VI). On trip it re-raises the last BlankVerdictError (if the loop
438:#       was blanking) else a RoleTransportError (both fail-loud; never a synthesized verdict).
439:#
440:# KILL-SWITCH (default-ON correct fix): PG_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG (default "1"). Set "0"/"false"/"no" to
441:# REVERT to the prior behavior — when OFF neither the ladder truncation nor the watchdog check ever
442:# fires, so control flow is byte-identical to before #1226. (Reliability-only kill-switch pattern.)
443:# Read LAZILY per-call (mirrors the adjacent PG_* knobs) so an override set after import is honored.
444:_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG_ENV = "PG_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG"
445:_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES_ENV = "PG_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES"
446:_ROLE_BLANK_MAX_RETRIES_DEFAULT = "3"
447:_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S_ENV = "PG_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S"
448:_ROLE_CALL_TIMEOUT_S_DEFAULT = "3600.0"
449:
450:
451:def role_blank_watchdog_enabled() -> bool:
452:    """Whether the #1226 blank-bound + wall-clock watchdog is ON (kill-switch, default ON).
453:
454:    `PG_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG` "0"/"false"/"no" -> OFF (revert to pre-#1226 behavior, byte-identical
455:    control flow); absent or anything else -> ON (the default-ON correct fix). LAW VI: env-driven,
456:    read lazily so a runtime override is honored.
457:    """
458:    token = os.getenv(_ROLE_BLANK_WATCHDOG_ENV, "1").strip().lower()
459:    return token not in ("0", "false", "no", "off")
